structured.research_question:
Can a paper-based electrochemical biosensor functionalized with anti-CRP
antibodies detect C-reactive protein in whole blood at clinically relevant
concentrations within 10 minutes, without sample preprocessing?
structured.subject: Whole blood samples (CRP analyte)
structured.independent:
Sensor format (paper-based electrochemical biosensor functionalized with
anti-CRP antibodies, vs. laboratory ELISA).
structured.dependent:
Detection sensitivity (limit of detection in mg/L) and time-to-result
(minutes).
structured.conditions:
Whole blood samples spiked with known CRP concentrations spanning 0.1-10
mg/L. No sample preprocessing (no centrifugation, no dilution). Room
temperature operation. Comparison against high-sensitivity ELISA.
structured.expected:
The paper biosensor detects CRP at concentrations below 0.5 mg/L within
10 minutes, matching laboratory ELISA sensitivity.

protocol.experiment_type: biosensor detection sensitivity and speed comparison

procedures.name: Biosensor Fabrication and Antibody Functionalization
procedures.intent: To prepare the paper-based electrochemical biosensors by integrating electrodes and functionalizing them with anti-CRP antibodies.

steps.title: Prepare Paper Substrate
steps.body_md: Cut chromatography paper (Whatman No. 1) into dimensions of 1 cm x 3 cm. Mark a fluidic channel approximately 2 mm wide along the length of each strip. Ensure consistent sizing for all substrates.
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact paper type if Whatman No.1 is not optimal for electrochemical applications (e.g., carbon-impregnated paper).
steps.notes: The paper dimensions are critical for sensor integration and fluidic control.

steps.title: Integrate Carbon Ink Electrodes
steps.body_md: Screen print or hand-draw (using conductive carbon ink pen) three electrodes onto each paper strip: a working electrode (WE), a counter electrode (CE), and a reference electrode (RE). The WE should be positioned within the marked fluidic channel. Ensure good electrical conductivity and precise electrode geometry. Cure the carbon ink according to manufacturer's instructions. A typical curing process involves heating at 80°C for 30 minutes.
equipment_needed: Screen printer (optional) or fine-tip conductive carbon ink pen
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact carbon ink formulation and curing instructions based on chosen ink.
Refine electrode geometry (e.g., diameters, spacing) for optimal electrochemical performance.
steps.notes: Carbon ink offers a cost-effective and flexible electrode material suitable for paper-based sensors.

steps.title: Apply Dielectric Layer
steps.body_md: Apply a hydrophobic dielectric layer (e.g., wax, SU-8 photoresist, or commercial dielectric ink) around the electrodes, leaving only the active surface of the WE, CE, and RE exposed. This layer defines the electrochemical cell area and prevents short-circuiting. Cure or dry the dielectric layer as per product specifications.
equipment_needed: Curing apparatus (if applicable, e.g., UV lamp for SU-8)
reagents_referenced: Hydrophobic dielectric material (e.g., wax emulsion, SU-8 photoresist, dielectric ink)
todo_for_researcher: Choose specific dielectric material and its corresponding application/curing protocol.
steps.notes: Proper encapsulation is critical for robust sensor performance and preventing signal interference.

steps.title: Clean Electrode Surface
steps.body_md: Rinse the fabricated paper biosensors with deionized water to remove any loose particles or contaminants from the electrode surfaces. Gently air-dry the sensors or use a low-flow nitrogen stream.
steps.notes: A clean surface is essential for effective antibody immobilization.

steps.title: Activate Carboxyl Groups on Electrode (if applicable)
steps.body_md: If using a carbon ink with carboxyl groups for EDC/NHS coupling, activate the carboxyl groups on the working electrode by incubating with a fresh solution of 100 mM NHS and 400 mM EDC in MES buffer (pH 5.5) for 30 minutes at room temperature. Rinse thoroughly with MES buffer (pH 5.5) to remove excess reagents.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm carbon ink composition to determine if carboxyl activation is required or if other functional groups are present for immobilization.
steps.notes: EDC/NHS coupling is a common method for covalently immobilizing antibodies onto carboxylated surfaces, ensuring stable attachment.

steps.title: Prepare Anti-CRP Antibody Solution
steps.body_md: Prepare a stock solution of anti-CRP antibodies (polyclonal or monoclonal, affinity-purified) at a concentration range of 5-50 µg/mL in 1X PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The optimal concentration for CRP detection sensitivity should be determined experimentally in a separate optimization study.
todo_for_researcher: Perform a preliminary optimization study to determine the exact optimal anti-CRP antibody concentration within the 5-50 µg/mL range for maximum sensitivity and minimum non-specific binding.
steps.notes: Antibody concentration is a critical parameter affecting sensor performance. High concentrations can lead to steric hindrance, while low concentrations may result in insufficient coverage.

steps.title: Immobilize Anti-CRP Antibodies on Working Electrode
steps.body_md: Drop-cast 5 µL of the prepared anti-CRP antibody solution (e.g., 20 µg/mL, if optimized) onto the active surface of the working electrode. Incubate the sensors in a humid chamber at room temperature for 2 hours to allow for antibody immobilization. Ensure the antibody solution remains on the electrode surface throughout the incubation.
controls: Negative control: sensor with no antibody immobilization, incubated with PBS buffer instead.
steps.notes: The humid chamber prevents evaporation of the antibody solution, ensuring uniform immobilization.

steps.title: Block Non-Specific Binding Sites
steps.body_md: After antibody immobilization, gently rinse the sensors with 1X PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Then, drop-cast 10 µL of a blocking solution (e.g., 10 mg/mL BSA in 1X PBS) onto the entire sensor area, covering all electrodes. Incubate for 1 hour at room temperature. This step blocks any remaining unoccupied binding sites on the electrode surface, minimizing non-specific adsorption of sample components.
todo_for_researcher: Optimize blocking agent and concentration (e.g., different proteins, detergents, or polymers) to ensure minimal non-specific binding with whole blood.
steps.notes: Effective blocking is critical for assays involving complex samples like whole blood to ensure high specificity.

steps.title: Wash and Dry Sensors
steps.body_md: Thoroughly wash the functionalized sensors with 1X PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to remove excess blocking agent. Perform three washes, each for 5 minutes, with gentle agitation. After washing, carefully air-dry the sensors or dry under a gentle nitrogen stream within the humid chamber context to prevent full dehydration.
equipment_needed: Stir plate with magnetic stirrer (optional for washing in bulk)
steps.notes: Proper washing prevents carryover of reagents that could interfere with downstream detection. Drying within a humid environment helps preserve antibody activity.

steps.title: Store Functionalized Biosensors
steps.body_md: Store the fully functionalized and dried biosensors in a sealed, desiccated container at 4°C until use. Label each container clearly with the date of fabrication.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the long-term stability of the functionalized sensors under specified storage conditions through stability studies.
steps.notes: Proper storage is essential to maintain the activity and integrity of the immobilized antibodies.

equipment: Curing apparatus (e.g., UV lamp if SU-8)
reagents: Hydrophobic dielectric material (e.g., wax emulsion, SU-8 photoresist, dielectric ink)

success_criteria.what: Visual integrity of electrodes and dielectric layer
success_criteria.how_measured: Microscopic inspection (low magnification)
success_criteria.threshold: No visible cracks, delamination, or misaligned features on ~95% of fabricated sensors.

success_criteria.how_measured: Measure resistance between connecting pads of individual electrodes (WE-CE, WE-RE) using a multimeter.
success_criteria.threshold: Resistance < 1 k Ohm for all pairs.

success_criteria.how_measured: XPS or FTIR analysis of a subset of functionalized sensors to confirm presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups.
success_criteria.threshold: Detection of characteristic peaks corresponding to protein (e.g. N 1s peak in XPS) on the working electrode surface.

success_criteria.how_measured: Electrochemical measurement (e.g., DPV or CV) of negative control sensors (no anti-CRP antibody) exposed to high concentration of non-target proteins (e.g., 100 mg/mL BSA in PBS).
success_criteria.threshold: Background signal (current) from negative controls should be less than 5% of the expected signal from target CRP at the lowest detection limit.

procedures.name: ELISA Standard Curve Generation
procedures.intent: To establish a robust standard curve for CRP detection using a commercially available high-sensitivity ELISA kit.

steps.title: Prepare CRP Standards
steps.body_md: Prepare a serial dilution of CRP standards according to the manufacturer's instructions for the high-sensitivity ELISA kit. The standard curve should span concentrations from 0 to 20 mg/L. Typically, this involves reconstituting a high-concentration standard and then performing serial dilutions using the provided diluent.

steps.title: Plate CRP Standards
steps.body_md: Carefully dispense each prepared CRP standard into appropriate wells of a 96-well ELISA plate in triplicate. Ensure accurate pipetting technique to minimize variability. Also, prepare triplicate wells for the zero standard (blank wells containing only diluent).
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume per well based on specific kit instructions
steps.notes: Maintaining triplicate samples for each concentration ensures robust data for curve fitting.

steps.title: Add Enzyme Conjugate
steps.body_md: Following the standard addition, add the enzyme conjugate as specified by the kit protocol to each well, including the standard and blank wells. Gently tap the plate to ensure thorough mixing.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume of enzyme conjugate based on specific kit instructions

steps.title: Incubate Plate
steps.body_md: Cover the ELISA plate to prevent evaporation and incubate at room temperature for the duration specified in the kit protocol. The recommended incubation time for CRP detection is typically around 30 minutes to 1 hour.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact incubation time and temperature from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Ensure stable room temperature conditions. Use an incubator if precise temperature control is critical or environmental fluctuations are significant.

steps.title: Wash Plate
steps.body_md: After incubation, carefully aspirate the contents of all wells. Wash the plate 3-5 times with the supplied wash buffer. Ensure complete aspiration of wash buffer at each step to minimize background signal, but avoid drying the wells completely.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact number of washes and wash buffer volume per well from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Improper washing is a common cause of high background in ELISA assays. Automated plate washers offer greater consistency.

steps.title: Add Substrate Solution
steps.body_md: Add the substrate solution to each well immediately after washing. The substrate will react with the enzyme conjugate to produce a quantifiable color change.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume of substrate solution based on specific kit instructions
steps.notes: Protect the substrate solution and the plate with added substrate from light, as many substrates are light-sensitive.

steps.title: Incubate with Substrate
steps.body_md: Incubate the plate in the dark at room temperature for the time recommended by the manufacturer. This incubation allows the color to develop. The typical incubation time is 10-30 minutes.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact substrate incubation time from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Monitor color development periodically if a recommended incubation time range is provided. Avoid over-incubation, which can lead to signal saturation.

steps.title: Add Stop Solution
steps.body_md: Add the stop solution to each well to terminate the enzymatic reaction and fix the color. This prevents further color development and stabilizes the signal for reading.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume of stop solution based on specific kit instructions
steps.notes: Add stop solution in the same order as the substrate to ensure consistent reaction times across the plate, especially if reading immediately after.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Within 5-15 minutes of adding the stop solution, read the absorbance of each well using a microplate reader set to the wavelength specified by the kit manufacturer (typically 450 nm). A reference wavelength (e.g., 620 nm) may also be used to correct for optical imperfections.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact primary and reference wavelengths from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Prompt reading after adding stop solution is important as some color reactions can fade over longer periods.

steps.title: Generate Standard Curve
steps.body_md: Plot the average absorbance values for each CRP standard concentration (including the blank) against their respective concentrations. Use appropriate curve-fitting software (e.g., 4-parameter logistic curve fit) to generate the standard curve. This curve will be used to determine the CRP concentration in unknown samples.
equipment_needed: Computer with curve-fitting software (e.g., GraphPad Prism, Excel)
todo_for_researcher: Select appropriate curve-fitting software and ensure staff proficiency
steps.notes: Evaluate the R-squared value and visual inspection of the generated curve to ensure a good fit and linearity within the relevant concentration range.

success_criteria.how_measured: Coefficient of determination (R-squared) from curve fitting software
Average absorbance of wells containing only diluent (blank)
success_criteria.threshold: < 0.15 OD (or as per kit specifications)

success_criteria.how_measured: Visual inspection of the curve; lowest non-zero standard has signal above blank, highest standard is not saturated.
success_criteria.threshold: Clear dose-response across 0-20 mg/L range, no saturation.

success_criteria.how_measured: Coefficient of Variation (CV) for absorbance readings of triplicate wells at each standard concentration

procedures.name: Blood Sample Preparation and Spiking
procedures.intent: To prepare whole blood samples by spiking with known concentrations of CRP to create a range of test samples relevant to clinical concentrations.

steps.title: Gather Reagents and Equipment
steps.body_md: Gather all necessary reagents and equipment including human whole blood (anticoagulant treated, e.g., EDTA or heparin), purified C-reactive protein (CRP) standard, sterile saline (0.9% NaCl), micropipettes, sterile pipette tips, blood collection tubes or similar sterile containers, a vortex mixer, and a timer. Ensure all materials are at room temperature prior to starting.
steps.notes: Using anticoagulant-treated blood is crucial to prevent clotting and ensures sample homogeneity during spiking and subsequent testing.

steps.title: Prepare Stock CRP Solution
steps.body_md: Dilute the purified C-reactive protein (CRP) standard in sterile saline (0.9% NaCl) to create a high-concentration stock solution. The concentration should be sufficiently high to allow for accurate serial dilutions to achieve the desired spike concentrations in whole blood, accounting for the dilution factor when adding to blood. Aim for a stock solution of at least 100 mg/L CRP, or higher if needed for subsequent dilutions.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm initial concentration of purified CRP standard and calculate appropriate amount for stock solution.
steps.notes: Ensure precise measurement to minimize variability in final spiked concentrations. Prepare this just before use.

steps.title: Prepare Intermediate CRP Dilutions
steps.body_md: From the high-concentration CRP stock solution, prepare a series of intermediate CRP dilutions in sterile saline (0.9% NaCl). These dilutions should be precisely calculated to allow for direct spiking into whole blood to achieve final concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/L with minimal volume addition (e.g., less than 5% of the total blood volume). For instance, if spiking 10 µL into 990 µL of blood, the spike solution should be 100x the target concentration.
todo_for_researcher: Calculate specific volumes and concentrations for intermediate CRP dilutions based on desired final blood concentrations and spike volume.
steps.notes: Minimize the volume of spike solution added to whole blood to avoid significant dilution of the blood sample itself. This is critical as per the hypothesis (no sample preprocessing, no dilution).

steps.title: Aliquot Whole Blood
steps.body_md: Dispense the pre-aliquoted anticoagulant-treated human whole blood into individual sterile tubes or vials. Each tube should contain a sufficient volume for one test (e.g., 1 mL). Ensure to include enough tubes for each target CRP concentration plus appropriate controls, with the specified number of replicates (e.g., 3-5 replicates per concentration).
equipment_needed: Sterile blood collection tubes or vials
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact number of replicates needed per condition based on experimental design.
steps.notes: Maintain sterility during aliquoting to prevent contamination. Gently invert the primary blood tube several times before aliquoting to ensure homogeneity.

steps.title: Prepare Negative Control (Unspiked Blood)
steps.body_md: For negative control samples, add an equivalent volume of sterile saline (0.9% NaCl) to designated tubes of whole blood, matching the volume of CRP spike solution used in the experimental samples (e.g., 10 µL). These samples will represent the baseline CRP level in the unspiked blood, which is assumed to be negligible or below the detection limit of the assays.
steps.notes: This control accounts for any endogenous CRP in the whole blood and the effect of adding a small volume of saline.

steps.title: Spike Whole Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Carefully add the pre-calculated volumes of intermediate CRP dilutions to the respective aliquoted whole blood samples, aiming for final CRP concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/L in the whole blood. Use a fresh pipette tip for each concentration and sample. Ensure the spike volume is minimal (e.g., <5% v/v of blood).
steps.notes: Precise pipetting is crucial. Work quickly to ensure consistency across samples.

steps.title: Homogenize Spiked Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Immediately after adding the CRP spike or saline, gently vortex each tube at a medium speed for 5-10 seconds to ensure thorough mixing of the CRP throughout the whole blood sample. Avoid excessive agitation that could cause hemolysis or foaming.
todo_for_researcher: Determine optimal vortex speed based on mixer and tube size to ensure thorough mixing without hemolysis.
steps.notes: Proper homogenization is key to ensuring that the spiked CRP is uniformly distributed within the blood sample, which is critical for accurate measurements.

steps.title: Incubate and Store Spiked Samples
steps.body_md: Keep the spiked whole blood samples at room temperature (20-25°C) for a maximum of 30 minutes before testing. This short incubation allows for complete diffusion and equilibrium of the spiked CRP within the blood matrix. Do not store samples for extended periods or at different temperatures to align with the 'room temperature operation' and 'no sample preprocessing' aspects of the hypothesis.
steps.notes: Strict adherence to room temperature and short incubation is vital to simulate point-of-care conditions and avoid degradation or instability of CRP in unpreserved whole blood.

reagents: Human whole blood (anticoagulant treated, e.g., EDTA or heparin)

success_criteria.how_measured: Spectrophotometric protein quantification (e.g., Bradford assay) on diluted stock
success_criteria.threshold: Within 5% of target concentration

success_criteria.how_measured: Visual inspection for clots or precipitates; consistency across replicate sensor readings (from downstream procedure)
success_criteria.threshold: No visible clots/precipitates; coefficient of variation (CV) for replicates below 10%

success_criteria.how_measured: Measurement of CRP in saline-spiked whole blood using the reference ELISA
success_criteria.threshold: Below the LOD of the reference ELISA

procedures.name: Biosensor CRP Detection
procedures.intent: To measure CRP concentrations in spiked whole blood samples using the fabricated paper-based electrochemical biosensor and record reaction times.

steps.title: Prepare Biosensor for Sample Application
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the fabricated paper-based electrochemical biosensor from its protective packaging. Ensure the working electrode is clean and free from any visible debris or damage. Place the biosensor on a flat, stable surface within a controlled environment (e.g., a clean bench).
todo_for_researcher: What specific protective packaging should be used for the biosensor to maintain its integrity prior to use?

steps.title: Calibrate Electrochemical Workstation
steps.body_md: Power on the electrochemical workstation. Before applying any sample, perform a standard instrument calibration according to the manufacturer's instructions. This typically involves connecting a dummy cell or using a known reference solution to verify electrochemical parameters like impedance or potential accuracy.
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact model of electrochemical workstation and its manufacturer's calibration procedure.
steps.notes: Ensuring proper calibration is critical for accurate and reproducible electrochemical measurements.

steps.title: Connect Biosensor to Workstation
steps.body_md: Carefully connect the prepared paper-based electrochemical biosensor to the electrochemical workstation using the appropriate leads. Ensure secure contact between the working, counter, and reference electrodes of the biosensor and the corresponding connectors of the workstation.
equipment_needed: Alligator clips or specific biosensor interface connectors
todo_for_researcher: Specify the type of connectors required for the biosensor (e.g., alligator clips, custom interface).
steps.notes: Poor electrical contact can lead to noisy or inaccurate data.

steps.title: Prepare Spiked Whole Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Ensure the whole blood samples spiked with known CRP concentrations (0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10 mg/L) are at room temperature (20-25°C). Gently invert each sample tube several times to ensure homogeneity immediately prior to use. Do not centrifuge or dilute. Also prepare a negative control (whole blood without spiked CRP) and a positive control (whole blood spiked with a high CRP concentration, e.g., 20 mg/L, if available).
reagents_referenced: Positive control (whole blood with high CRP)
controls: Positive control (whole blood spiked with high CRP)
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the specific range for 'room temperature' in the laboratory.
Specify the exact source and preparation method for the whole blood used for spiking, including anti-coagulant.
steps.notes: Maintaining temperature consistency and homogeneity is crucial for reliable results, especially without sample preprocessing. The positive control helps confirm sensor functionality.

steps.title: Apply Sample to Biosensor
steps.body_md: Pipet 20 µL of the first spiked whole blood sample directly onto the active sensing area of the paper-based biosensor. Ensure the sample completely covers the working electrode and makes good contact with all three electrodes (working, counter, reference). Start a timer immediately upon sample application.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the precise 'active sensing area' for sample application on the specific biosensor design.
Confirm optimal lighting conditions to minimize photoelectrochemical interference if applicable.
steps.notes: The exact volume may need optimization, but 20 µL is a common starting point for paper-based devices. Immediate timing is essential for kinetic measurements.

steps.title: Initiate Electrochemical Measurement
steps.body_md: Apply the electrochemical detection method (e.g., Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) or Chronoamperometry) immediately after sample application. For EIS, typically apply a small alternating potential (e.g., 5-10 mV RMS) over a frequency range (e.g., 1 Hz to 100 kHz) at a chosen DC potential (e.g., 0 V vs. reference). For chronoamperometry, apply a fixed potential (e.g., -0.2 V or +0.2 V vs. reference) and record current over time. Record all relevant electrochemical data.
todo_for_researcher: Precisely define the optimized electrochemical parameters (applied DC potential, AC amplitude, frequency range for EIS, or applied fixed potential for chronoamperometry) for the specific biosensor anti-CRP functionalization.
Specify the data acquisition rate for recording electrochemical signals.
steps.notes: The choice of electrochemical method and specific parameters should be optimized for the biosensor's functionalization and expected analyte. Data acquisition should be continuous for the full measurement duration.

steps.title: Record Reaction Time and Endpoint Data
steps.body_md: Continuously monitor and record the electrochemical response from the workstation for up to 10 minutes. Note the time at which a stable signal or a significant change (indicating binding kinetics or reaching equilibrium) is observed. Record the final electrochemical impedance or current measurements at the 10-minute mark.
todo_for_researcher: Define what constitutes a 'stable signal' or 'significant change' for the specific chosen electrochemical method (e.g., percentage change over time, slope threshold).
steps.notes: The measurement duration is critical to assess the time-to-result, and capturing the full kinetic profile (if applicable) is important.

steps.title: Clean Biosensor and Repeat for Other Samples
steps.body_md: After each measurement, carefully disconnect the biosensor from the workstation. Gently rinse the biosensor with deionized water and dry it, or prepare a new biosensor for the next sample if single-use. Repeat steps 5-7 for all remaining spiked whole blood samples, negative control, and positive control. Ensure to use a fresh biosensor or thoroughly cleaned and validated biosensor for each measurement to avoid cross-contamination or carry-over effects.
todo_for_researcher: Clarify if the biosensor is single-use or reusable. If reusable, specify the cleaning validation protocol.
Specify the number of replicates for each CRP concentration and control measurement.
steps.notes: Replication is essential for statistical robustness. Consistency in the cleaning or replacement of sensors between measurements is paramount.

steps.title: Data Analysis (Initial Review)
steps.body_md: Upon completion of all measurements, perform an initial review of the collected electrochemical data. Look for clear trends correlating CRP concentration with the measured electrochemical signal (e.g., impedance changes, current responses). Visually inspect for any anomalies or outliers in the data. Export raw data for later in-depth analysis.
equipment_needed: Spreadsheet software (e.g., Excel) or data plotting software
todo_for_researcher: Specify the preferred file format for exporting raw data.
Outline the initial plotting and data visualization strategy.
steps.notes: This initial review helps identify potential issues early and guides further adjustments or troubleshooting.

equipment: Alligator clips or specific biosensor interface connectors
Spreadsheet software (e.g., Excel) or data plotting software
reagents: Positive control (whole blood with high CRP)

success_criteria.what: Electrochemical signal response to CRP concentration
success_criteria.how_measured: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) impedance change or Chronoamperometry current response
success_criteria.threshold: A consistent, dose-dependent change in signal across the tested CRP concentration range (0.1-10 mg/L) above the negative control baseline.

success_criteria.how_measured: Time taken to achieve a stable electrochemical signal or significant binding event post-sample application.
success_criteria.threshold: ≤ 10 minutes

success_criteria.how_measured: Electrochemical signal for unspiked whole blood.
success_criteria.threshold: Signal similar to baseline/non-specific binding, significantly lower than 0.1 mg/L CRP spiked sample.

success_criteria.how_measured: Electrochemical signal for whole blood spiked with high CRP concentration.
success_criteria.threshold: Highest signal among all samples, confirming sensor saturation/robustness.

procedures.name: ELISA CRP Detection
procedures.intent: To measure CRP concentrations in the same spiked whole blood samples using the high-sensitivity ELISA kit as a gold standard comparison.

steps.title: Prepare Reagents
steps.body_md: Allow all reagents from the high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit to reach room temperature (20-25 °C) before use. Gently mix all reagents by inversion or swirling, avoiding foam formation. Carefully read the kit insert for specific preparation instructions for each reagent, especially concerning reconstitution of lyophilized components.
steps.notes: Ensure proper mixing of all reagents, especially reconstituted components, to guarantee uniformity.

steps.title: Dilute Whole Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Prepare dilutions of the spiked whole blood samples according to the high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit instructions. As per the experimental hypothesis, whole blood samples are used, so specific dilution factors for whole blood (not plasma or serum) must be followed. Typically, this involves a multi-step dilution process to bring CRP concentrations within the linear range of the assay. For instance, a 1:10 dilution followed by a 1:100 dilution may be necessary, depending on expected maximum CRP concentrations and kit sensitivity.

other.sample_dilution_factor: as per ELISA kit instructions for whole blood

todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact recommended dilution factor for whole blood samples. If not explicitly stated for whole blood, assume use of a similar matrix like serum or plasma as a guideline and note the deviation later, or contact the manufacturer for clarification. Document the exact dilution scheme used.
steps.notes: Accurate dilution is critical for obtaining reliable results within the assay's detection range. The hypothesis explicitly states 'no sample preprocessing' for the paper sensor, but for ELISA, dilution is an inherent sample processing step to fall within the assay's linear range and reduce matrix effects.

steps.title: Plate Standards, Controls, and Samples
steps.body_md: Add 100 μL of each reconstituted standard, control, and diluted sample to appropriate wells of the provided ELISA plate. Ensure to run standards in duplicate or triplicate for accurate curve fitting, and controls in duplicate. Assign wells systematically to avoid errors.
steps.notes: Carefully record the plate layout to avoid mix-ups during analysis.

steps.title: First Incubation (Analyte Binding)
steps.body_md: Cover the ELISA plate with an adhesive plate sealer and incubate at room temperature (20-25 °C) for the duration specified in the kit insert. Avoid exposing the plate to direct light during incubation.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact incubation time for the analyte binding step.

steps.title: Wash Plate (First Wash)
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the plate sealer. Aspirate the contents of each well completely without scratching the well bottom. Wash each well 3-5 times with 300 μL of prepared washing buffer. Ensure complete aspiration between washes to remove unbound material. Pay attention to the plate edges and corners.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact number of washes recommended for this step (typically 3-5).
steps.notes: Thorough washing is crucial to minimize background signal and ensure assay specificity.

steps.title: Add Detection Conjugate
steps.body_md: Add 100 μL of the prepared detection conjugate (e.g., HRP-labeled anti-CRP antibody) to each well. Ensure complete coverage of the well bottom.

steps.title: Second Incubation (Conjugate Binding)
steps.body_md: Cover the ELISA plate with a fresh adhesive plate sealer and incubate at room temperature (20-25 °C) for the duration specified in the kit insert. Avoid exposing the plate to direct light during incubation.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact incubation time for the conjugate binding step.

steps.title: Wash Plate (Second Wash)
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the plate sealer. Aspirate the contents of each well completely. Wash each well 3-5 times with 300 μL of prepared washing buffer, ensuring complete aspiration between washes. Invert the plate and tap it firmly on absorbent paper to remove any residual wash buffer.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact number of washes recommended for this step (typically 3-5).
steps.notes: Adequate washing is critical to reduce background signal from unbound conjugate.

steps.title: Add Substrate Solution
steps.body_md: Add 100 μL of the prepared substrate solution (e.g., TMB) to each well. Avoid exposing the substrate to light, as it is often light-sensitive.

steps.title: Third Incubation (Color Development)
steps.body_md: Incubate the plate at room temperature (20-25 °C) in the dark for the duration specified in the kit insert. Monitor color development visually, but do not allow over-development, as this can lead to saturation of the signal.
equipment_needed: Plate sealer (opaque, or cover with aluminum foil)
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact recommended incubation time for color development.
steps.notes: Color development time is critical for accurate quantification and can vary based on kit lot and temperature.

steps.title: Add Stop Solution
steps.body_md: Add 50 μL of stop solution (e.g., sulfuric acid) to each well. Mix gently by tapping the plate. The color should change from blue to yellow (for TMB substrate). Add the stop solution in the same order and at the same rate as the substrate solution to ensure consistent reaction times across the plate.
steps.notes: Exercise caution when handling stop solution, as it is often acidic. Wear appropriate PPE.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Read the absorbance of each well at the detection wavelength specified in the kit insert within 30 minutes after adding the stop solution. If a dual wavelength reading is possible, set the reference wavelength as recommended (e.g., 620 nm).
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact primary and optional reference detection wavelengths.
steps.notes: Read the plate as quickly as possible and within the recommended timeframe after stopping the reaction to prevent signal decay or drift.

steps.title: Generate Standard Curve and Calculate Concentrations
steps.body_md: Using appropriate curve-fitting software (e.g., 4-parameter logistic regression), generate a standard curve from the absorbance values of the standards. Interpolate the CRP concentrations of the controls and diluted samples from this standard curve. Account for the sample dilution factor to obtain the final CRP concentration in the original whole blood samples.
todo_for_researcher: Select appropriate curve-fitting software and ensure it is properly calibrated for ELISA data analysis.
steps.notes: Ensure the calculated concentrations for controls fall within their specified ranges, indicating assay validity.

procedures.name: Data Analysis and Performance Comparison
procedures.intent: To analyze the biosensor and ELISA data to determine detection sensitivity (LoD) and time-to-result, and compare performance metrics.

steps.title: Compile Biosensor Raw Data
steps.body_md: Collect all raw electrochemical response data from the biosensor measurements. This includes current or voltage readings corresponding to each CRP concentration for every replicate. Organize this data in a structured format, such as a spreadsheet, ensuring clear labeling of CRP concentration, replicate number, and measured signal.

steps.title: Calculate Mean and Standard Deviation for Biosensor Data
steps.body_md: For each CRP concentration point tested by the biosensor, calculate the mean signal response and the standard deviation across all replicates. These values will be used for calibration curve generation and LoD calculation.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet software or statistical software

steps.title: Generate Biosensor Calibration Curve
steps.body_md: Plot the mean biosensor signal response against the corresponding spiked CRP concentrations. Fit an appropriate regression model (e.g., linear, sigmoidal) to the data to generate a calibration curve. This curve will be essential for determining the limit of detection (LoD).
equipment_needed: Computer with graphing and statistical software
steps.notes: Ensure the fitted model provides a good R-squared value, indicating a strong correlation between signal and concentration.

steps.title: Calculate Biosensor Limit of Detection (LoD)
steps.body_md: Calculate the Limit of Detection (LoD) for the biosensor using the formula: LoD = (3 * Standard Deviation of Blank) / Slope of the calibration curve. The 'blank' refers to the signal obtained from unprocessed whole blood without spiked CRP. The slope is derived from the linear portion of the calibration curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
steps.notes: Ensure an adequate number of blank measurements were taken during the experimental phase to provide a reliable standard deviation.

steps.title: Record Biosensor Time-to-Result
steps.body_md: Document the time taken from the application of the whole blood sample to the display of the final electrochemical reading for the biosensor. This should reflect the practical operational time for the device, excluding any preparation steps.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm whether the recorded time includes any brief incubation/reaction time on the sensor surface prior to measurement.
steps.notes: The hypothesis specifies a target of 'within 10 minutes', so this measurement is crucial.

steps.title: Compile ELISA Data
steps.body_md: Retrieve all quantitative results from the high-sensitivity ELISA measurements. This includes the CRP concentration values obtained for each spiked whole blood sample. Organize this data in a structured format, such as a spreadsheet, alongside the corresponding biosensor data for easy comparison.

steps.title: Calculate ELISA Limit of Detection (LoD)
steps.body_md: Determine the Limit of Detection (LoD) for the ELISA system using its established method, typically based on the signal from blank samples (e.g., 3 * Standard Deviation of Blank / Slope of the standard curve). Obtain this information from the ELISA kit manufacturer's specifications or calculate it from experimental data of the standard curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
todo_for_researcher: Precisely define the 'blank' for ELISA measurements, considering the whole blood matrix.

steps.title: Record ELISA Time-to-Result
steps.body_md: Document the total time required to perform the ELISA procedure, from sample preparation (if any) through to obtaining the final quantitative results. Be mindful that ELISA typically involves multiple incubation and wash steps.
todo_for_researcher: Clarify if 'time-to-result' for ELISA includes sample incubation time on the plate or just analysis time.
steps.notes: ELISA typically has a much longer time-to-result than rapid sensing methods.

steps.title: Compare LoDs of Biosensor and ELISA
steps.body_md: Directly compare the calculated LoD of the paper-based biosensor with the LoD of the high-sensitivity ELISA. Evaluate if the biosensor's LoD meets or surpasses the performance of the ELISA, particularly in the context of the clinically relevant range (below 0.5 mg/L).

steps.title: Compare Time-to-Result of Biosensor and ELISA
steps.body_md: Compare the documented time-to-result for the paper-based biosensor with that of the ELISA. Assess whether the biosensor achieves its result within the target 10-minute window and if this is a significant improvement over the ELISA.

steps.title: Perform Statistical Analysis (if applicable)
steps.body_md: If appropriate for the dataset (e.g., comparing mean values across different concentrations or sensor types), perform statistical tests such as t-tests or ANOVA to determine if observed differences in sensitivity or accuracy between the biosensor and ELISA are statistically significant. Use a significance level of p < 0.05.
todo_for_researcher: Determine specific statistical tests based on data distribution and experimental design characteristics.
steps.notes: This step helps validate the significance of any observed performance differences.

steps.title: Generate Performance Comparison Report
steps.body_md: Compile all findings into a comprehensive report. Include biosensor and ELISA LoD values, time-to-result, calibration curves, and any statistical analysis results. Discuss the implications of the findings regarding the biosensor's suitability for rapid, point-of-care CRP detection in whole blood without preprocessing.
equipment_needed: Computer with word processing and graphing software
steps.notes: This report will directly address the research question and hypothesis.

steps.title: Prepare Paper Substrate
steps.body_md: Cut chromatography paper (Whatman No. 1) into dimensions of 1 cm x 3 cm. Mark a fluidic channel approximately 2 mm wide along the length of each strip. Ensure consistent sizing for all substrates.
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact paper type if Whatman No.1 is not optimal for electrochemical applications (e.g., carbon-impregnated paper).
steps.notes: The paper dimensions are critical for sensor integration and fluidic control.

steps.title: Integrate Carbon Ink Electrodes
steps.body_md: Screen print or hand-draw (using conductive carbon ink pen) three electrodes onto each paper strip: a working electrode (WE), a counter electrode (CE), and a reference electrode (RE). The WE should be positioned within the marked fluidic channel. Ensure good electrical conductivity and precise electrode geometry. Cure the carbon ink according to manufacturer's instructions. A typical curing process involves heating at 80°C for 30 minutes.
equipment_needed: Screen printer (optional) or fine-tip conductive carbon ink pen
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact carbon ink formulation and curing instructions based on chosen ink.
Refine electrode geometry (e.g., diameters, spacing) for optimal electrochemical performance.
steps.notes: Carbon ink offers a cost-effective and flexible electrode material suitable for paper-based sensors.

steps.title: Apply Dielectric Layer
steps.body_md: Apply a hydrophobic dielectric layer (e.g., wax, SU-8 photoresist, or commercial dielectric ink) around the electrodes, leaving only the active surface of the WE, CE, and RE exposed. This layer defines the electrochemical cell area and prevents short-circuiting. Cure or dry the dielectric layer as per product specifications.
equipment_needed: Curing apparatus (if applicable, e.g., UV lamp for SU-8)
reagents_referenced: Hydrophobic dielectric material (e.g., wax emulsion, SU-8 photoresist, dielectric ink)
todo_for_researcher: Choose specific dielectric material and its corresponding application/curing protocol.
steps.notes: Proper encapsulation is critical for robust sensor performance and preventing signal interference.

steps.title: Clean Electrode Surface
steps.body_md: Rinse the fabricated paper biosensors with deionized water to remove any loose particles or contaminants from the electrode surfaces. Gently air-dry the sensors or use a low-flow nitrogen stream.
steps.notes: A clean surface is essential for effective antibody immobilization.

steps.title: Activate Carboxyl Groups on Electrode (if applicable)
steps.body_md: If using a carbon ink with carboxyl groups for EDC/NHS coupling, activate the carboxyl groups on the working electrode by incubating with a fresh solution of 100 mM NHS and 400 mM EDC in MES buffer (pH 5.5) for 30 minutes at room temperature. Rinse thoroughly with MES buffer (pH 5.5) to remove excess reagents.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm carbon ink composition to determine if carboxyl activation is required or if other functional groups are present for immobilization.
steps.notes: EDC/NHS coupling is a common method for covalently immobilizing antibodies onto carboxylated surfaces, ensuring stable attachment.

steps.title: Prepare Anti-CRP Antibody Solution
steps.body_md: Prepare a stock solution of anti-CRP antibodies (polyclonal or monoclonal, affinity-purified) at a concentration range of 5-50 µg/mL in 1X PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The optimal concentration for CRP detection sensitivity should be determined experimentally in a separate optimization study.
todo_for_researcher: Perform a preliminary optimization study to determine the exact optimal anti-CRP antibody concentration within the 5-50 µg/mL range for maximum sensitivity and minimum non-specific binding.
steps.notes: Antibody concentration is a critical parameter affecting sensor performance. High concentrations can lead to steric hindrance, while low concentrations may result in insufficient coverage.

steps.title: Immobilize Anti-CRP Antibodies on Working Electrode
steps.body_md: Drop-cast 5 µL of the prepared anti-CRP antibody solution (e.g., 20 µg/mL, if optimized) onto the active surface of the working electrode. Incubate the sensors in a humid chamber at room temperature for 2 hours to allow for antibody immobilization. Ensure the antibody solution remains on the electrode surface throughout the incubation.
controls: Negative control: sensor with no antibody immobilization, incubated with PBS buffer instead.
steps.notes: The humid chamber prevents evaporation of the antibody solution, ensuring uniform immobilization.

steps.title: Block Non-Specific Binding Sites
steps.body_md: After antibody immobilization, gently rinse the sensors with 1X PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Then, drop-cast 10 µL of a blocking solution (e.g., 10 mg/mL BSA in 1X PBS) onto the entire sensor area, covering all electrodes. Incubate for 1 hour at room temperature. This step blocks any remaining unoccupied binding sites on the electrode surface, minimizing non-specific adsorption of sample components.
todo_for_researcher: Optimize blocking agent and concentration (e.g., different proteins, detergents, or polymers) to ensure minimal non-specific binding with whole blood.
steps.notes: Effective blocking is critical for assays involving complex samples like whole blood to ensure high specificity.

steps.title: Wash and Dry Sensors
steps.body_md: Thoroughly wash the functionalized sensors with 1X PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to remove excess blocking agent. Perform three washes, each for 5 minutes, with gentle agitation. After washing, carefully air-dry the sensors or dry under a gentle nitrogen stream within the humid chamber context to prevent full dehydration.
equipment_needed: Stir plate with magnetic stirrer (optional for washing in bulk)
steps.notes: Proper washing prevents carryover of reagents that could interfere with downstream detection. Drying within a humid environment helps preserve antibody activity.

steps.title: Store Functionalized Biosensors
steps.body_md: Store the fully functionalized and dried biosensors in a sealed, desiccated container at 4°C until use. Label each container clearly with the date of fabrication.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the long-term stability of the functionalized sensors under specified storage conditions through stability studies.
steps.notes: Proper storage is essential to maintain the activity and integrity of the immobilized antibodies.

steps.title: Prepare CRP Standards
steps.body_md: Prepare a serial dilution of CRP standards according to the manufacturer's instructions for the high-sensitivity ELISA kit. The standard curve should span concentrations from 0 to 20 mg/L. Typically, this involves reconstituting a high-concentration standard and then performing serial dilutions using the provided diluent.

steps.title: Plate CRP Standards
steps.body_md: Carefully dispense each prepared CRP standard into appropriate wells of a 96-well ELISA plate in triplicate. Ensure accurate pipetting technique to minimize variability. Also, prepare triplicate wells for the zero standard (blank wells containing only diluent).
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume per well based on specific kit instructions
steps.notes: Maintaining triplicate samples for each concentration ensures robust data for curve fitting.

steps.title: Add Enzyme Conjugate
steps.body_md: Following the standard addition, add the enzyme conjugate as specified by the kit protocol to each well, including the standard and blank wells. Gently tap the plate to ensure thorough mixing.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume of enzyme conjugate based on specific kit instructions

steps.title: Incubate Plate
steps.body_md: Cover the ELISA plate to prevent evaporation and incubate at room temperature for the duration specified in the kit protocol. The recommended incubation time for CRP detection is typically around 30 minutes to 1 hour.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact incubation time and temperature from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Ensure stable room temperature conditions. Use an incubator if precise temperature control is critical or environmental fluctuations are significant.

steps.title: Wash Plate
steps.body_md: After incubation, carefully aspirate the contents of all wells. Wash the plate 3-5 times with the supplied wash buffer. Ensure complete aspiration of wash buffer at each step to minimize background signal, but avoid drying the wells completely.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact number of washes and wash buffer volume per well from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Improper washing is a common cause of high background in ELISA assays. Automated plate washers offer greater consistency.

steps.title: Add Substrate Solution
steps.body_md: Add the substrate solution to each well immediately after washing. The substrate will react with the enzyme conjugate to produce a quantifiable color change.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume of substrate solution based on specific kit instructions
steps.notes: Protect the substrate solution and the plate with added substrate from light, as many substrates are light-sensitive.

steps.title: Incubate with Substrate
steps.body_md: Incubate the plate in the dark at room temperature for the time recommended by the manufacturer. This incubation allows the color to develop. The typical incubation time is 10-30 minutes.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact substrate incubation time from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Monitor color development periodically if a recommended incubation time range is provided. Avoid over-incubation, which can lead to signal saturation.

steps.title: Add Stop Solution
steps.body_md: Add the stop solution to each well to terminate the enzymatic reaction and fix the color. This prevents further color development and stabilizes the signal for reading.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal volume of stop solution based on specific kit instructions
steps.notes: Add stop solution in the same order as the substrate to ensure consistent reaction times across the plate, especially if reading immediately after.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Within 5-15 minutes of adding the stop solution, read the absorbance of each well using a microplate reader set to the wavelength specified by the kit manufacturer (typically 450 nm). A reference wavelength (e.g., 620 nm) may also be used to correct for optical imperfections.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm exact primary and reference wavelengths from the specific ELISA kit instructions
steps.notes: Prompt reading after adding stop solution is important as some color reactions can fade over longer periods.

steps.title: Generate Standard Curve
steps.body_md: Plot the average absorbance values for each CRP standard concentration (including the blank) against their respective concentrations. Use appropriate curve-fitting software (e.g., 4-parameter logistic curve fit) to generate the standard curve. This curve will be used to determine the CRP concentration in unknown samples.
equipment_needed: Computer with curve-fitting software (e.g., GraphPad Prism, Excel)
todo_for_researcher: Select appropriate curve-fitting software and ensure staff proficiency
steps.notes: Evaluate the R-squared value and visual inspection of the generated curve to ensure a good fit and linearity within the relevant concentration range.

steps.title: Gather Reagents and Equipment
steps.body_md: Gather all necessary reagents and equipment including human whole blood (anticoagulant treated, e.g., EDTA or heparin), purified C-reactive protein (CRP) standard, sterile saline (0.9% NaCl), micropipettes, sterile pipette tips, blood collection tubes or similar sterile containers, a vortex mixer, and a timer. Ensure all materials are at room temperature prior to starting.
steps.notes: Using anticoagulant-treated blood is crucial to prevent clotting and ensures sample homogeneity during spiking and subsequent testing.

steps.title: Prepare Stock CRP Solution
steps.body_md: Dilute the purified C-reactive protein (CRP) standard in sterile saline (0.9% NaCl) to create a high-concentration stock solution. The concentration should be sufficiently high to allow for accurate serial dilutions to achieve the desired spike concentrations in whole blood, accounting for the dilution factor when adding to blood. Aim for a stock solution of at least 100 mg/L CRP, or higher if needed for subsequent dilutions.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm initial concentration of purified CRP standard and calculate appropriate amount for stock solution.
steps.notes: Ensure precise measurement to minimize variability in final spiked concentrations. Prepare this just before use.

steps.title: Prepare Intermediate CRP Dilutions
steps.body_md: From the high-concentration CRP stock solution, prepare a series of intermediate CRP dilutions in sterile saline (0.9% NaCl). These dilutions should be precisely calculated to allow for direct spiking into whole blood to achieve final concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/L with minimal volume addition (e.g., less than 5% of the total blood volume). For instance, if spiking 10 µL into 990 µL of blood, the spike solution should be 100x the target concentration.
todo_for_researcher: Calculate specific volumes and concentrations for intermediate CRP dilutions based on desired final blood concentrations and spike volume.
steps.notes: Minimize the volume of spike solution added to whole blood to avoid significant dilution of the blood sample itself. This is critical as per the hypothesis (no sample preprocessing, no dilution).

steps.title: Aliquot Whole Blood
steps.body_md: Dispense the pre-aliquoted anticoagulant-treated human whole blood into individual sterile tubes or vials. Each tube should contain a sufficient volume for one test (e.g., 1 mL). Ensure to include enough tubes for each target CRP concentration plus appropriate controls, with the specified number of replicates (e.g., 3-5 replicates per concentration).
equipment_needed: Sterile blood collection tubes or vials
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact number of replicates needed per condition based on experimental design.
steps.notes: Maintain sterility during aliquoting to prevent contamination. Gently invert the primary blood tube several times before aliquoting to ensure homogeneity.

steps.title: Prepare Negative Control (Unspiked Blood)
steps.body_md: For negative control samples, add an equivalent volume of sterile saline (0.9% NaCl) to designated tubes of whole blood, matching the volume of CRP spike solution used in the experimental samples (e.g., 10 µL). These samples will represent the baseline CRP level in the unspiked blood, which is assumed to be negligible or below the detection limit of the assays.
steps.notes: This control accounts for any endogenous CRP in the whole blood and the effect of adding a small volume of saline.

steps.title: Spike Whole Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Carefully add the pre-calculated volumes of intermediate CRP dilutions to the respective aliquoted whole blood samples, aiming for final CRP concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/L in the whole blood. Use a fresh pipette tip for each concentration and sample. Ensure the spike volume is minimal (e.g., <5% v/v of blood).
steps.notes: Precise pipetting is crucial. Work quickly to ensure consistency across samples.

steps.title: Homogenize Spiked Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Immediately after adding the CRP spike or saline, gently vortex each tube at a medium speed for 5-10 seconds to ensure thorough mixing of the CRP throughout the whole blood sample. Avoid excessive agitation that could cause hemolysis or foaming.
todo_for_researcher: Determine optimal vortex speed based on mixer and tube size to ensure thorough mixing without hemolysis.
steps.notes: Proper homogenization is key to ensuring that the spiked CRP is uniformly distributed within the blood sample, which is critical for accurate measurements.

steps.title: Incubate and Store Spiked Samples
steps.body_md: Keep the spiked whole blood samples at room temperature (20-25°C) for a maximum of 30 minutes before testing. This short incubation allows for complete diffusion and equilibrium of the spiked CRP within the blood matrix. Do not store samples for extended periods or at different temperatures to align with the 'room temperature operation' and 'no sample preprocessing' aspects of the hypothesis.
steps.notes: Strict adherence to room temperature and short incubation is vital to simulate point-of-care conditions and avoid degradation or instability of CRP in unpreserved whole blood.

steps.title: Prepare Biosensor for Sample Application
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the fabricated paper-based electrochemical biosensor from its protective packaging. Ensure the working electrode is clean and free from any visible debris or damage. Place the biosensor on a flat, stable surface within a controlled environment (e.g., a clean bench).
todo_for_researcher: What specific protective packaging should be used for the biosensor to maintain its integrity prior to use?

steps.title: Calibrate Electrochemical Workstation
steps.body_md: Power on the electrochemical workstation. Before applying any sample, perform a standard instrument calibration according to the manufacturer's instructions. This typically involves connecting a dummy cell or using a known reference solution to verify electrochemical parameters like impedance or potential accuracy.
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact model of electrochemical workstation and its manufacturer's calibration procedure.
steps.notes: Ensuring proper calibration is critical for accurate and reproducible electrochemical measurements.

steps.title: Connect Biosensor to Workstation
steps.body_md: Carefully connect the prepared paper-based electrochemical biosensor to the electrochemical workstation using the appropriate leads. Ensure secure contact between the working, counter, and reference electrodes of the biosensor and the corresponding connectors of the workstation.
equipment_needed: Alligator clips or specific biosensor interface connectors
todo_for_researcher: Specify the type of connectors required for the biosensor (e.g., alligator clips, custom interface).
steps.notes: Poor electrical contact can lead to noisy or inaccurate data.

steps.title: Prepare Spiked Whole Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Ensure the whole blood samples spiked with known CRP concentrations (0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10 mg/L) are at room temperature (20-25°C). Gently invert each sample tube several times to ensure homogeneity immediately prior to use. Do not centrifuge or dilute. Also prepare a negative control (whole blood without spiked CRP) and a positive control (whole blood spiked with a high CRP concentration, e.g., 20 mg/L, if available).
reagents_referenced: Positive control (whole blood with high CRP)
controls: Positive control (whole blood spiked with high CRP)
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the specific range for 'room temperature' in the laboratory.
Specify the exact source and preparation method for the whole blood used for spiking, including anti-coagulant.
steps.notes: Maintaining temperature consistency and homogeneity is crucial for reliable results, especially without sample preprocessing. The positive control helps confirm sensor functionality.

steps.title: Apply Sample to Biosensor
steps.body_md: Pipet 20 µL of the first spiked whole blood sample directly onto the active sensing area of the paper-based biosensor. Ensure the sample completely covers the working electrode and makes good contact with all three electrodes (working, counter, reference). Start a timer immediately upon sample application.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the precise 'active sensing area' for sample application on the specific biosensor design.
Confirm optimal lighting conditions to minimize photoelectrochemical interference if applicable.
steps.notes: The exact volume may need optimization, but 20 µL is a common starting point for paper-based devices. Immediate timing is essential for kinetic measurements.

steps.title: Initiate Electrochemical Measurement
steps.body_md: Apply the electrochemical detection method (e.g., Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) or Chronoamperometry) immediately after sample application. For EIS, typically apply a small alternating potential (e.g., 5-10 mV RMS) over a frequency range (e.g., 1 Hz to 100 kHz) at a chosen DC potential (e.g., 0 V vs. reference). For chronoamperometry, apply a fixed potential (e.g., -0.2 V or +0.2 V vs. reference) and record current over time. Record all relevant electrochemical data.
todo_for_researcher: Precisely define the optimized electrochemical parameters (applied DC potential, AC amplitude, frequency range for EIS, or applied fixed potential for chronoamperometry) for the specific biosensor anti-CRP functionalization.
Specify the data acquisition rate for recording electrochemical signals.
steps.notes: The choice of electrochemical method and specific parameters should be optimized for the biosensor's functionalization and expected analyte. Data acquisition should be continuous for the full measurement duration.

steps.title: Record Reaction Time and Endpoint Data
steps.body_md: Continuously monitor and record the electrochemical response from the workstation for up to 10 minutes. Note the time at which a stable signal or a significant change (indicating binding kinetics or reaching equilibrium) is observed. Record the final electrochemical impedance or current measurements at the 10-minute mark.
todo_for_researcher: Define what constitutes a 'stable signal' or 'significant change' for the specific chosen electrochemical method (e.g., percentage change over time, slope threshold).
steps.notes: The measurement duration is critical to assess the time-to-result, and capturing the full kinetic profile (if applicable) is important.

steps.title: Clean Biosensor and Repeat for Other Samples
steps.body_md: After each measurement, carefully disconnect the biosensor from the workstation. Gently rinse the biosensor with deionized water and dry it, or prepare a new biosensor for the next sample if single-use. Repeat steps 5-7 for all remaining spiked whole blood samples, negative control, and positive control. Ensure to use a fresh biosensor or thoroughly cleaned and validated biosensor for each measurement to avoid cross-contamination or carry-over effects.
todo_for_researcher: Clarify if the biosensor is single-use or reusable. If reusable, specify the cleaning validation protocol.
Specify the number of replicates for each CRP concentration and control measurement.
steps.notes: Replication is essential for statistical robustness. Consistency in the cleaning or replacement of sensors between measurements is paramount.

steps.title: Data Analysis (Initial Review)
steps.body_md: Upon completion of all measurements, perform an initial review of the collected electrochemical data. Look for clear trends correlating CRP concentration with the measured electrochemical signal (e.g., impedance changes, current responses). Visually inspect for any anomalies or outliers in the data. Export raw data for later in-depth analysis.
equipment_needed: Spreadsheet software (e.g., Excel) or data plotting software
todo_for_researcher: Specify the preferred file format for exporting raw data.
Outline the initial plotting and data visualization strategy.
steps.notes: This initial review helps identify potential issues early and guides further adjustments or troubleshooting.

steps.title: Prepare Reagents
steps.body_md: Allow all reagents from the high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit to reach room temperature (20-25 °C) before use. Gently mix all reagents by inversion or swirling, avoiding foam formation. Carefully read the kit insert for specific preparation instructions for each reagent, especially concerning reconstitution of lyophilized components.
steps.notes: Ensure proper mixing of all reagents, especially reconstituted components, to guarantee uniformity.

steps.title: Dilute Whole Blood Samples
steps.body_md: Prepare dilutions of the spiked whole blood samples according to the high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit instructions. As per the experimental hypothesis, whole blood samples are used, so specific dilution factors for whole blood (not plasma or serum) must be followed. Typically, this involves a multi-step dilution process to bring CRP concentrations within the linear range of the assay. For instance, a 1:10 dilution followed by a 1:100 dilution may be necessary, depending on expected maximum CRP concentrations and kit sensitivity.

other.sample_dilution_factor: as per ELISA kit instructions for whole blood

todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact recommended dilution factor for whole blood samples. If not explicitly stated for whole blood, assume use of a similar matrix like serum or plasma as a guideline and note the deviation later, or contact the manufacturer for clarification. Document the exact dilution scheme used.
steps.notes: Accurate dilution is critical for obtaining reliable results within the assay's detection range. The hypothesis explicitly states 'no sample preprocessing' for the paper sensor, but for ELISA, dilution is an inherent sample processing step to fall within the assay's linear range and reduce matrix effects.

steps.title: Plate Standards, Controls, and Samples
steps.body_md: Add 100 μL of each reconstituted standard, control, and diluted sample to appropriate wells of the provided ELISA plate. Ensure to run standards in duplicate or triplicate for accurate curve fitting, and controls in duplicate. Assign wells systematically to avoid errors.
steps.notes: Carefully record the plate layout to avoid mix-ups during analysis.

steps.title: First Incubation (Analyte Binding)
steps.body_md: Cover the ELISA plate with an adhesive plate sealer and incubate at room temperature (20-25 °C) for the duration specified in the kit insert. Avoid exposing the plate to direct light during incubation.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact incubation time for the analyte binding step.

steps.title: Wash Plate (First Wash)
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the plate sealer. Aspirate the contents of each well completely without scratching the well bottom. Wash each well 3-5 times with 300 μL of prepared washing buffer. Ensure complete aspiration between washes to remove unbound material. Pay attention to the plate edges and corners.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact number of washes recommended for this step (typically 3-5).
steps.notes: Thorough washing is crucial to minimize background signal and ensure assay specificity.

steps.title: Add Detection Conjugate
steps.body_md: Add 100 μL of the prepared detection conjugate (e.g., HRP-labeled anti-CRP antibody) to each well. Ensure complete coverage of the well bottom.

steps.title: Second Incubation (Conjugate Binding)
steps.body_md: Cover the ELISA plate with a fresh adhesive plate sealer and incubate at room temperature (20-25 °C) for the duration specified in the kit insert. Avoid exposing the plate to direct light during incubation.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact incubation time for the conjugate binding step.

steps.title: Wash Plate (Second Wash)
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the plate sealer. Aspirate the contents of each well completely. Wash each well 3-5 times with 300 μL of prepared washing buffer, ensuring complete aspiration between washes. Invert the plate and tap it firmly on absorbent paper to remove any residual wash buffer.
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact number of washes recommended for this step (typically 3-5).
steps.notes: Adequate washing is critical to reduce background signal from unbound conjugate.

steps.title: Add Substrate Solution
steps.body_md: Add 100 μL of the prepared substrate solution (e.g., TMB) to each well. Avoid exposing the substrate to light, as it is often light-sensitive.

steps.title: Third Incubation (Color Development)
steps.body_md: Incubate the plate at room temperature (20-25 °C) in the dark for the duration specified in the kit insert. Monitor color development visually, but do not allow over-development, as this can lead to saturation of the signal.
equipment_needed: Plate sealer (opaque, or cover with aluminum foil)
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact recommended incubation time for color development.
steps.notes: Color development time is critical for accurate quantification and can vary based on kit lot and temperature.

steps.title: Add Stop Solution
steps.body_md: Add 50 μL of stop solution (e.g., sulfuric acid) to each well. Mix gently by tapping the plate. The color should change from blue to yellow (for TMB substrate). Add the stop solution in the same order and at the same rate as the substrate solution to ensure consistent reaction times across the plate.
steps.notes: Exercise caution when handling stop solution, as it is often acidic. Wear appropriate PPE.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Read the absorbance of each well at the detection wavelength specified in the kit insert within 30 minutes after adding the stop solution. If a dual wavelength reading is possible, set the reference wavelength as recommended (e.g., 620 nm).
todo_for_researcher: Consult the specific ELISA kit insert for the exact primary and optional reference detection wavelengths.
steps.notes: Read the plate as quickly as possible and within the recommended timeframe after stopping the reaction to prevent signal decay or drift.

steps.title: Generate Standard Curve and Calculate Concentrations
steps.body_md: Using appropriate curve-fitting software (e.g., 4-parameter logistic regression), generate a standard curve from the absorbance values of the standards. Interpolate the CRP concentrations of the controls and diluted samples from this standard curve. Account for the sample dilution factor to obtain the final CRP concentration in the original whole blood samples.
todo_for_researcher: Select appropriate curve-fitting software and ensure it is properly calibrated for ELISA data analysis.
steps.notes: Ensure the calculated concentrations for controls fall within their specified ranges, indicating assay validity.

steps.title: Compile Biosensor Raw Data
steps.body_md: Collect all raw electrochemical response data from the biosensor measurements. This includes current or voltage readings corresponding to each CRP concentration for every replicate. Organize this data in a structured format, such as a spreadsheet, ensuring clear labeling of CRP concentration, replicate number, and measured signal.

steps.title: Calculate Mean and Standard Deviation for Biosensor Data
steps.body_md: For each CRP concentration point tested by the biosensor, calculate the mean signal response and the standard deviation across all replicates. These values will be used for calibration curve generation and LoD calculation.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet software or statistical software

steps.title: Generate Biosensor Calibration Curve
steps.body_md: Plot the mean biosensor signal response against the corresponding spiked CRP concentrations. Fit an appropriate regression model (e.g., linear, sigmoidal) to the data to generate a calibration curve. This curve will be essential for determining the limit of detection (LoD).
equipment_needed: Computer with graphing and statistical software
steps.notes: Ensure the fitted model provides a good R-squared value, indicating a strong correlation between signal and concentration.

steps.title: Calculate Biosensor Limit of Detection (LoD)
steps.body_md: Calculate the Limit of Detection (LoD) for the biosensor using the formula: LoD = (3 * Standard Deviation of Blank) / Slope of the calibration curve. The 'blank' refers to the signal obtained from unprocessed whole blood without spiked CRP. The slope is derived from the linear portion of the calibration curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
steps.notes: Ensure an adequate number of blank measurements were taken during the experimental phase to provide a reliable standard deviation.

steps.title: Record Biosensor Time-to-Result
steps.body_md: Document the time taken from the application of the whole blood sample to the display of the final electrochemical reading for the biosensor. This should reflect the practical operational time for the device, excluding any preparation steps.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm whether the recorded time includes any brief incubation/reaction time on the sensor surface prior to measurement.
steps.notes: The hypothesis specifies a target of 'within 10 minutes', so this measurement is crucial.

steps.title: Compile ELISA Data
steps.body_md: Retrieve all quantitative results from the high-sensitivity ELISA measurements. This includes the CRP concentration values obtained for each spiked whole blood sample. Organize this data in a structured format, such as a spreadsheet, alongside the corresponding biosensor data for easy comparison.

steps.title: Calculate ELISA Limit of Detection (LoD)
steps.body_md: Determine the Limit of Detection (LoD) for the ELISA system using its established method, typically based on the signal from blank samples (e.g., 3 * Standard Deviation of Blank / Slope of the standard curve). Obtain this information from the ELISA kit manufacturer's specifications or calculate it from experimental data of the standard curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
todo_for_researcher: Precisely define the 'blank' for ELISA measurements, considering the whole blood matrix.

steps.title: Record ELISA Time-to-Result
steps.body_md: Document the total time required to perform the ELISA procedure, from sample preparation (if any) through to obtaining the final quantitative results. Be mindful that ELISA typically involves multiple incubation and wash steps.
todo_for_researcher: Clarify if 'time-to-result' for ELISA includes sample incubation time on the plate or just analysis time.
steps.notes: ELISA typically has a much longer time-to-result than rapid sensing methods.

steps.title: Compare LoDs of Biosensor and ELISA
steps.body_md: Directly compare the calculated LoD of the paper-based biosensor with the LoD of the high-sensitivity ELISA. Evaluate if the biosensor's LoD meets or surpasses the performance of the ELISA, particularly in the context of the clinically relevant range (below 0.5 mg/L).

steps.title: Compare Time-to-Result of Biosensor and ELISA
steps.body_md: Compare the documented time-to-result for the paper-based biosensor with that of the ELISA. Assess whether the biosensor achieves its result within the target 10-minute window and if this is a significant improvement over the ELISA.

steps.title: Perform Statistical Analysis (if applicable)
steps.body_md: If appropriate for the dataset (e.g., comparing mean values across different concentrations or sensor types), perform statistical tests such as t-tests or ANOVA to determine if observed differences in sensitivity or accuracy between the biosensor and ELISA are statistically significant. Use a significance level of p < 0.05.
todo_for_researcher: Determine specific statistical tests based on data distribution and experimental design characteristics.
steps.notes: This step helps validate the significance of any observed performance differences.

steps.title: Generate Performance Comparison Report
steps.body_md: Compile all findings into a comprehensive report. Include biosensor and ELISA LoD values, time-to-result, calibration curves, and any statistical analysis results. Discuss the implications of the findings regarding the biosensor's suitability for rapid, point-of-care CRP detection in whole blood without preprocessing.
equipment_needed: Computer with word processing and graphing software
steps.notes: This report will directly address the research question and hypothesis.

assumptions: Availability of a dedicated Biosafety Level 1 (BSL-1) or BSL-2 laboratory for handling human blood samples.
Access to standard analytical lab equipment (e.g., potentiostat, microplate reader, pipettes, centrifuges).
Availability of commercial high-purity human CRP antigen for spiking.
Availability of commercial anti-CRP antibodies suitable for biosensor functionalization.
Trained personnel for handling biological samples and operating electrochemical equipment.
